{"step_by_step_thinking": "In Down syndrome patients, periodontal disease is commonly observed, and specific dental anomalies are associated with the condition. Document [3] (Title: Embryology, Teeth -- Pathophysiology) states that taurodontism, characterized by enlarged pulp chambers and apically positioned furcations, is frequently seen in Down syndrome and affects molars. It specifically notes that molars are the most commonly affected teeth in taurodontism. This condition is linked to periodontal complications due to the altered tooth structure. Additionally, Document [13] (Title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Oral Gingiva -- Clinical Significance) confirms that genetic disorders such as Down syndrome increase the risk of gingival and periodontal disease. While the anterior teeth may be affected in terms of morphology (e.g., peg laterals), the primary periodontal pathology in Down syndrome is associated with the molars. Among the options, the molars are more commonly affected, and the lower molars are particularly noted in the literature for being vulnerable to severe periodontitis in Down syndrome. Therefore, based on the evidence, the lower molar is the most likely to have severe periodontitis.", "answer_choice": "D"}
